revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:зЃ°еҰље±±еЏ¤еұі]][[zh:зЃ°еҰље±±еЏ¤еұі]]
{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Hazukayama Kofun | image = {{image requested|cat=е–ње¤љж–№её‚}} | part_of = {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|е®‡е†…йқ’жөӨеЏ¤еұізң¤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|qq=}} | location = {{ill|Kitakata, Fukushima|en|Kitakata, Fukushima|qid=Q496506|lt=Kitakata|1=Kitakata, Fukushima}}, Keitoku Town, Shingu, Oyamakoshi 2908-1, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37.623367|N|139.829928|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | omitted = JP-07 | type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=ZenpЕҚkЕҚenfun}} | area = Mound length 61.2 m | constructed = 5th century | burial = 1 box-type Sarcophagus, 2 wooden coffins | buried = | burial goods = Human remains, numerous burial items | notes = None | map = Japan Fukushima | map_type = Keyhole-shaped Kofun-Northeast | map_dot_label = Hazukayama Kofun | map_dot_label = bottom | architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=ZenpЕҚkЕҚenfun|2=ja|3=е‰Қж–№еңЊе††еұі|4=ko|5=м „л°©н›„м›ђл¶„|6=zh|7=е‰Қж–№еңЊењ“еұі}} }}
{{nihongo|''''Hazukayama Kofun''''|ja|2=зЃ°еҰље±±еЏ¤еұі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hazukayama Kofun|qq=}} is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=ZenpЕҚkЕҚenfun}} located in {{ill|Kitakata, Fukushima|en|Kitakata, Fukushima|qid=Q496506|lt=Kitakata|1=Kitakata, Fukushima}}, Fukushima Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|е®‡е†…йқ’жөӨеЏ¤еұізң¤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|qq=}}, and while it has not been Historic Site1 as a historic site, it is a significant archaeological feature.
== Overview == Hazukayama Kofun is located on the terminal slopes at the western edge of the {{ill|Aizu Basin|ja|дәљжөӨз›†ењ°|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu Basin|1=Aizu Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11381254}} in Fukushima Prefecture's western region<ref name="5thSurveyReport2016"/>. It is the northernmost tumulus within the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|е®‡е†…йқ’жөӨеЏ¤еұізң¤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|qq=}}<ref name="5thSurveyReport2016"/>. Several excavations have been conducted by {{ill|Tohoku Gakuin University|en|Tohoku Gakuin University|qid=Q910529|lt=Tohoku Gakuin University|1=Tohoku Gakuin University}}<ref name="5thSurveyReport2016"/>.
The tumulus has a keyhole-shaped mound with the front section facing northeast. Its total length is 61.2 meters, making it the second largest tumulus in the group, following the {{ill|Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|lt=Kamegamori Kofun|ja|дғЂгѓ¶жӘ®еЏ¤еұі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamegamori-Chinjumori Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to дғЂгѓ¶жӘ®гѓ»йҺ®е®€жӘ®еЏ¤еұі}} (127.3 meters)<ref name="5thSurveyReport2016"/>. burial include one box-type Sarcophagus and two wooden coffins. The Sarcophagus yielded human remains, and both coffins contained burial items such as bronze mirrors and a large number of iron burial goods<ref name="2016Report"/><ref name="2017Report"/>. Notably, the human remains were exceptionally well-preserved<ref name="2017Report"/>.
The tumulus is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}<ref name="2016Report"/>. The discovery of numerous iron burial goods marks this site as the first of its kind in the Tohoku region for tumuli from this period<ref name="2016Report"/>. Conventional views suggest that the Aizu region's connections with the Yamato Kingdom weakened from the early to mid-Kofun period. However, the iron burial goods and the use of a box-type Sarcophagus at this site suggest strong ties to the Yamato Kingdom, challenging these assumptions<ref name="2017Report"/>.
A nearby archaeological site, believed to be the residence of a powerful local leader during the Kofun period, supports the theory that the individual buried in Hazukayama Kofun was the head of this local polity<ref name="2017Report"/>. During the medieval period, the eastern part of the mound became the burial site of the Shingu clan, who built {{ill|ShingЕ« Castle|en|ShingЕ« Castle|qid=Q11501731|lt=Shingu Castle (Mutsu Province)|1=ShingЕ« Castle}} (a Historic Site1 historic site). In the Edo period, a stone-paved Kofun (kyЕҚzuka) was added to the rear section<ref name="5thSurveyReport2016"/>.
== History of Excavations == * 1986: Survey conducted by the {{ill|Fukushima Museum|en|Fukushima Museum|qid=Q4352239|lt=Fukushima Prefectural Museum|1=Fukushima Museum}}. * 20dd: First excavation by Tohoku Gakuin University's Faculty of Letters, report published in 2012<ref name="1stSurveyReport2012"/>. * 2012: Second excavation by Tohoku Gakuin University, report published in 2013<ref name="2ndSurveyReport2013"/>. * 2013: Third excavation by Tohoku Gakuin University, report published in 2014<ref name="3rdSurveyReport2014"/>. * 2014: Fourth excavation by Tohoku Gakuin University, report published in 2015<ref name="4thSurveyReport2015"/>. * 2015: Fifth excavation by Tohoku Gakuin University, report published in 2016<ref name="5thSurveyReport2016"/>. * 2016: Sixth and seventh excavations by Tohoku Gakuin University, reports published in 2017 and 2018<ref name="2016Report"/>.
== area == The mound's area are as follows<ref name="5thSurveyReport2016"/>: * Total length: 61.2 meters * Rear section diameter: 33.2 meters * Front section length: 27.6 meters
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == {{small|Sources used in the article}} * "Hazukayama Kofun," *Fukushima Prefectural Museum Excavation Survey Report No. 16*, 1987. * {{Cite journal|author=|title=[http://www.tohoku-gakuin.ac.jp/research/journal/bk2012/pdf/bk2012no05_03.pdf First Excavation Report of Hazukayama Kofun, Kitakata City, Fukushima Prefecture]|date=2012|publisher=Tohoku Gakuin University Academic Research Association|journal=Tohoku Gakuin University Historical and Cultural Journal|pages=15-39|ref=<2012Report>}}.
== Related Reading == {{small|Additional references not used directly}} * {{Cite journal|author=|title=Hazukayama Kofun's Ninth Excavation Report|date=2019|publisher=Tohoku Gakuin University|journal=Tohoku Gakuin University Historical and Cultural Journal|pages=1-47}}.
